retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Equipment necessary for OMT techniques to treat TMJ dysfunction include: An exam table An OMT table A massage table An alternative location that comforts the patient in sitting allows the physician to perform the technique comfortably.

retrieved_docs.title: Labial Adhesions -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A few weeks of topical management is recommended before surgical management is considered. Surgical lysis of the fusion is recommended if topical management is unsuccessful. The surgical management is usually performed under general anesthesia and consists of gentle traction.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Adherences and Adhesions
retrieved_docs.content: An adherence represents a brief hold of the articular surfaces. Adhesion can happen between the condyle and disc or the disc and fossa. Adhesions result from a fibrous connective tissue or loss of lubrication between the structures. It is characterized by restriction in the normal translation of the condyle movement with no pain. In chronic situations, the patient senses an inability to get the teeth back to occlusion during the closure.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Autologous Blood Injection: This is a conservative technique in which the joint space receives an injection with the patient's whole blood. In this technique, the joint space is lavaged with crystalloid fluid and drained. Whole blood is then drawn from the patient and injected into the joint space. The volume of blood injected is approximately 2 to 4 mL into the joint space and 1 to 1.5 mL into the pericapsular structures. The goal of injecting autologous blood into the joint space is to cause an inflammatory reaction resulting in fibrosis and scarring of the joint and capsular tissue. This process will decrease compliance of the joint and decreased range of motion. There are reports regarding concerns about damage to the joint cartilage secondary to the inflammatory reaction using this technique. [27] [2] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Labial Adhesions -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Recurrences are common in labial adhesions, regardless of the mode of treatment used. Labial adhesions may keep reforming until the female patient goes through puberty. Some studies report a rate of recurrence from 11% to 14% with either topical or surgical management. Recurrences may be managed with topical treatment or with surgical lysis of the fusion. Another treatment modality includes manual separation with a continuation of proper hygiene and cleanliness.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Alveolar Osteitis
retrieved_docs.content: More recently, low-level laser therapy (LLLT) has been suggested to be an effective alternative treatment with comparable results to other known treatments with the benefit of being both safe and inexpensive. [7] With some studies showing this newer technology to be both effective and advantageous, with further study, it could become a protocolized consensus treatment in the future.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Botulinum Toxin A Injection: This can be used with other techniques or as primary therapy. Twenty-five to fifty units of botulinum toxin A is injected directly into the lateral pterygoid to prevent recurrent dislocation. Injections can be repeated every 3 to 6 months to improve outcomes and reduce morbidity. [1] [12] The mechanism of action blocks the release of acetylcholine of the neuromuscular junction by blocking the calcium-mediated release. The result of blocking the release of acetylcholine is a temporary weakening of the muscle. The use of the toxin has been reported to be a reasonable, safe treatment option for use in the pediatric population with recurrent TMJ dislocation. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Structural Incompatibility with Articular Surfaces
retrieved_docs.content: The disorder results from changes in the smooth sliding surfaces of the TMJ. The alteration causes friction stickiness and inhibits joint function. Structural incompatibility classifies as a deviation in form, adhesions, subluxation, and spontaneous dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Invasive Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Invasive strategies include intra-articular long-acting corticosteroid, hyaluronic acid, and botox injections. These interventions are recommended once conservative therapies have failed or in severe acute exacerbations.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, Facial Laceration Repair -- Technique or Treatment -- Special Considerations for Suture Choice
retrieved_docs.content: Scars will continue to mature for approximately 12 months; avoiding sunlight for that period will help prevent hyperpigmentation. If scars appear excessively erythematous in the initial 2 to 3 weeks postrepair, treatment with a pulsed-dye laser will help to decrease the redness. Raised or widened scars may be treated with steroid injections or potentially with ablative lasers like carbon dioxide or erbium: YAG lasers. Some patients, particularly those predisposed to keloid development, may also benefit from silicone sheeting or topical gel, which should be used consistently for the first year after injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Administer antiviral +/- antibacterial prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Invasive Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Intra-articular steroids are indicated in the acute treatment of osteoarthritis of the TMJ, but multiple doses can lead to the destruction of articular cartilage. [21] [22] There is only limited evidence regarding the efficacy of hyaluronic acid injections in treating acute exacerbations. [23] Botulinum toxin injections are used to treat painful trigger points and chronic bruxism, but a recent Cochrane study found inconclusive evidence for myofascial pain. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A detailed history and physical examination are obtained prior to proceeding with ablative laser therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Obtain informed consent and review indications, intended outcomes, anticipated recovery course, and risks of the procedure with the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Direct MFR is mainly used to stretch and minimize muscle tightness, yet its analgesic effect is noteworthy. In a cytochemical investigation, scientists discovered intercellular fibroblasts responding by secreting inflammatory cytokines, surpassing hyperplasia, and modifying cell shape and alignment. An in vitro study showed that MFR-led strain release for 60 seconds reversed inflammatory effects in previously strained cells. Current trials have shown that MFR may promote wound healing by affecting the extracellular matrix.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent and delivery of vertex presentation are both classified as child procedures within the broader category of surgical manipulation, indicating that these specific interventions are considered subtypes of surgical manipulation in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1988
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on joints are classified as other operations on joint structures, indicating that these therapeutic interventions are grouped within the broader category of joint operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on joints is a classification that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on joints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent is categorized as a child concept within surgical manipulation, indicating that the delivery of therapeutic agents can be performed as a specific type of surgical intervention. This relationship underscores that certain therapeutic applications are integrated into operative procedures, reflecting their role as a subset of surgical techniques in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier encompasses several procedures that are currently considered trendy in medical practice, including بتر (amputation), المعالجة بالأدوية (drug therapy), صورة المفصل الشعاعية (radiographic imaging of the joint), البزل (aspiration or biopsy), and التخثير الحراري (thermal coagulation). These interventions are recognized as contemporary options that clinicians may select when appropriate, reflecting current preferences and advancements in treatment and diagnostic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative interventions that includes specific techniques such as Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position. Application of Therapeutic Agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or other therapeutic substances, while Manual Conversion of Position involves the physical repositioning of tissues, organs, or skeletal structures during a procedure. Both Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position are considered child concepts within Surgical Manipulation, illustrating the diverse range of actions that fall under this overarching surgical domain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: The repair of retinal tear; detachment is described as having a broader relationship with thermal ablation (الإنفاذ الحراري), indicating that thermal ablation techniques are broadly relevant to the management and treatment of retinal tears and detachments in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2698
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a therapeutic procedure that is performed directly on the nose (nos) and serves as the clinical focus of the Arabic term الرُعاف, which denotes a nosebleed.
